item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm seconding the issue with the lack of explanation about how this ship got into our timeline/universe. I was very confused when the alternate world documentations was presented, and I had to scroll up a few times to confirm the setting. Sadly, I found no clarification.

This SCP is extremely well-written, but it lacks what usually constitutes an SCP article. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How it made it here isn't relevant to the story being told. This is a modern day ghost ship with a tragic story. The only voices that matter are the voices of the dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ships and sometimes other vehicles are traditionally referred to as female, though I’m not sure why historically. It wasn’t done for humor, it’s a normal thing that sailors and other people who work closely with ships do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, the few issues I had were the strange usage of the gender-neutral pronouns for the leader of the MTF, which was really confusing at first and I had to look it up to make sense of it, but in the first exploration log, said MTF leader then apparently engenders the ship as a “her” (if this was done purely out of humor, this complaint can be disregarded).
The second issue is that there’s no clear explanation as to how this vessel made its way to our timeline. The nukes went off and…then transdimensional warp happened? Maybe linking to another dimension-hopping Skip could be linked to its discovery?
I think it was otherwise written very well, for how grim it is. The logs felt very human and were hard to not sympathize with. I’m currently leaving as a no vote for the moment, ultimately.

item.title: No offense meant
item.content:encoded: I just discovered that the site does not alert you when someone replies to a comment, so my bad for not seeing this reply till just today. I wanted to say I meant no offense by continuing the pickle joke. I meant to use that phrase to note that, even though this a very well written story (seriously, I cannot emphasize this point enough: it is fantastically written), my own personal dislike of nihilistic stories and ideas of "what is an SCP vs. what is an SCP Tale" get in the way of me enjoying the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The intense horror setting in as you, the reader, realize that all this work and effort was a lie and all those people were going to die anyway is palpable.

Though my natural inclination is to want to know more, about the war, about how the sub transferred into the foundation’s universe… I completely appreciate why that isn’t explored here. In fact, I quite love the unsettling dread the story produces in context of a world I know very little about… leaving me with intense sadness at knowing a world died and we know just enough about it to know how it differed from ours, and nothing more.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First and foremost, not everything needs to be a mystery. The purpose of this story isn't about the mystery, it's about the death of hope.

+ Spoilers
– hide block

Not everything gets wrapped up in neat little packages. The Foundation may never know which dimension this comes from, there are uncountable amounts of permutations through the multiverse, and this was just one of them.

In my headcanon there are uncountable mysteries in the Universe and while the Foundation spends trillions every year on research, not all of them will ever be explored.

I wanted to tell a story about how people persevered, tried, and ultimately failed. The guilt and shame of carrying the weight of all that expectation, of knowing the hope given is absolutely false, and yet keeping up appearances anyway? This was the point of story.

The war was lost long before the Far Horizon ever set out and everyone knew that. The whole Far Horizon project was a last ditch effort to try and save someone, anyone. But even that was a pipe dream, because there weren't enough people that could be saved to make the project worthwhile.

EVEN THEN, the "lottery" was a lie and the "carefully designed list" of people who would go was mired in corruption and desperation. Everyone who got on that boat knew that, but they got on anyway.

This isn't a mystery story. It isn't a story about good triumphing over evil, or even evil triumphing over good. It is a story about greed, corruption, desperation, hope, and the wholesale nihilistic reality that all of it was a lie from the very beginning.

It most certainly wasn't written to "tickle your pickle", and I resent that phrase being used in just about every way it is possible for me to resent it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with this sentiment in full, with an extra helping of praising how well the story was written, even if the pickle remains depressed and moody. Judging by the author's post above, I believe that is the point. I have only minor quibbles beyond what DrLevy posted and my own personal question of "Why you so good at making me feel so sad??"

+ show block
– hide block

I feel that there isn't any mystery left in this story. In you're very successful effort to write a "dark and depressing tale…" you gave us all the information we can reasonably expect about the object, it's crew, and even its "cargo." You could point to the allusions to a war that never happened as being the main mystery, but as DrLevy succinctly put it, that isn't enough were the focus of the story lies, so the reader can easily forget or simply not care enough about it. I also feel like exploring some aspect of the trans-dimensional nature of the sub would have been appropriate, as the Foundation would probably want to be as clear as possible as to possible origins.

item.title: Re: Why
item.content:encoded: That is pretty much exactly as I see it. They'd already given people hope. It would have been cruel beyond belief to take away that last single thing from so many that had already lost so much.

item.title: Why
item.content:encoded:
Just.. why did they build it?

Did they think they could build the cryo chambers, but couldn’t, and didn’t have the heart to tell everyone that they had failed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean yeah, really very strong. The build towards the end is especially moving and the final addendum has a lot great stuff going on. There's a fair bit of practiced nuance in this piece too, I think, that I really appreciate.

Plus, it's a submarine from the Harry Potter universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read this last night, gave it a +1 and enjoyed it so much that I came back this morning and read it again.

One really minor error I spotted:

the UK Hydrgraphic Office

You're missing an 'o' in Hydrographic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a fantastically dark SCP that I really enjoy, even if it perhaps takes a little too long to get there.

There's some really evocative about the way this progresses and the bleakness of it that makes this great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love this. To be honest, I wasn't expecting anything other than to enjoy it, but I wasn't expecting it to create such a stark and depressing atmosphere so expertly. The way the story slowly sinks into the bleakest aspects is so well done that you don't really notice it happening until it's already over.

This is a wonderful article that I really enjoyed. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea doesn't really tickle my pickle I'm afraid. I feel like this is another "artifact from another timeline where x has happened".

The execution is great though, so no vote.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I want to thank DrAkimoto for helping me by going over this dark and depressing tale, and for being such an incredible friend. Thank you for your support and your friendship, it means a lot.

If you liked this and want to read more from me, please visit my author page.

+ Attribution
– hide block
